svg.title: IB Chemistry HL Organic Chemistry Mind Map
svg.text: IB Chemistry HL — Organic Chemistry
Structure 3.2 Functional groups & classification; Reactivity 3.3-3.4 Electron sharing / electron-pair sharing reactions
ORGANIC
CHEMISTRY
HL IB Chemistry
FORMULAS & MOLECULAR MODELS
Structure 3.2.1
Empirical, molecular, structural (full & condensed) formulas
Skeletal formulas and stereochemical formulas
Interconvert molecular, skeletal & structural formulas
Construct 3D models (real or virtual) of organic molecules
FUNCTIONAL GROUPS
Structure 3.2.2
Halogeno, hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, alkoxy groups
Amino, amido, ester and phenyl groups
Functional group gives characteristic physical/chemical properties
Classify compounds as saturated or unsaturated
HOMOLOGOUS SERIES & TRENDS
Structure 3.2.3-3.2.4
Alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, halogenoalkanes, alcohols
Aldehydes, ketones, carboxylic acids, ethers
Amines, amides and esters as separate series
Explain mp/bp trends: chain length, branching, IMF strength
IUPAC NOMENCLATURE
Structure 3.2.5
Systematic naming rules of IUPAC for organic compounds
Name saturated/mono-unsaturated chains up to 6 carbons
One functional group per name: halogeno, hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl
Include straight-chain and branched-chain isomers
STRUCTURAL ISOMERISM
Structure 3.2.6
Same molecular formula, different connectivity
Chain, position and functional-group isomers
Classify primary, secondary & tertiary alcohols
Classify primary, secondary & tertiary halogenoalkanes and amines
RADICAL SUBSTITUTION REACTIONS
Reactivity 3.3.1-3.3.3
Radical = species with an unpaired electron; highly reactive
Homolytic fission of halogens by UV light or heat (initiation)
Propagation and termination steps of the chain reaction
Free-radical substitution of alkanes by halogens; product mixtures
Alkanes: kinetically stable (strong C-C/C-H, non-polar) bonds
NUCLEOPHILES & ELECTROPHILES (SL)
Reactivity 3.4.1-3.4.5
Nucleophile donates an electron pair to form a new bond
Electrophile accepts an electron pair from its partner
Heterolytic fission: both electrons stay with one fragment
SN reactions of halogenoalkanes with nucleophiles (equations only)
Electrophilic addition of alkenes with water, halogens, HX (equations only)
STEREOISOMERISM
Structure 3.2.7 (HL)
Stereoisomers: same connectivity, different spatial arrangement
Cis-trans isomerism in non-cyclic alkenes, C3/C4 cycloalkanes
Chiral carbon: tetrahedral arrangement, wedge-dash formulas
Enantiomers: non-superimposable mirror images (3D modelling)
Optical activity, racemic mixtures, chiral environments
STRUCTURAL ELUCIDATION: MS, IR, NMR
Structure 3.2.8-3.2.12 (HL)
Mass spectrometry: fragmentation patterns, molecular ion
IR spectra: functional-group region, characteristic wavenumbers
IR link: greenhouse gases and absorption of IR radiation
1H NMR: chemical environments, chemical shift, integration
Splitting patterns: singlet, doublet, triplet, quartet
Combine multiple analytical techniques to solve a structure
LEWIS ACIDS/BASES & COMPLEX IONS
Reactivity 3.4.6-3.4.8 (HL)
Lewis acid = electron-pair acceptor; Lewis base = electron-pair donor
Nucleophiles are Lewis bases; electrophiles are Lewis acids
Coordination bond forms when a Lewis base reacts with a Lewis acid
Ligands donate electron pairs to transition-metal cations
Deduce charge on a complex ion from ligands present
SN1 vs SN2 MECHANISMS
Reactivity 3.4.9-3.4.10 (HL)
SN2: concerted, one-step mechanism for primary halogenoalkanes
SN1: two-step mechanism (carbocation) for tertiary halogenoalkanes
Secondary halogenoalkanes: both SN1 and SN2 can occur
SN2 is stereospecific (inversion of configuration)
Rate depends on leaving-group identity: RCl vs RBr vs RI
ELECTROPHILIC ADDITION MECHANISMS
Reactivity 3.4.11-3.4.12 (HL)
Alkenes: high electron density of C=C attracts electrophiles
Full curly-arrow mechanism with halogens, water, hydrogen halides
Relative stability of carbocation intermediates
Markovnikov's rule predicts the major product
ELECTROPHILIC SUBSTITUTION OF BENZENE
Reactivity 3.4.13 (HL)
Benzene reacts with charged electrophiles, E+
Delocalized ring resists addition; favours substitution
Mechanism: electrophile attack, arenium intermediate, H+ loss
Example: nitration of benzene using HNO3/H2SO4
Core content (SL & HL)
HL-only content (AHL)
IB Chemistry 2025 syllabus — Structure 3.2 & Reactivity 3.3/3.4
